Hands-on_Ex07A

Author

Persis Evangeline

Published

May 15, 2026

Modified

June 5, 2026

This exercise is about learning to model, analyse and visualise network data using R.

Learning objectives:

  1. create graph object data frames

  2. build network graph visualisation

  3. compute network geometrics

  4. build advanced graph visualisation by incorporating the network geometrics

  5. build interactive network visualisation

Getting started

pacman::p_load(igraph, tidygraph, ggraph, 
               visNetwork, lubridate, clock,
               tidyverse, graphlayouts, 
               concaveman, ggforce)

Data

2 datasets are being used. One is containing nodes data while other contains edge(aka link) data.

GAStech_nodes <- read_csv("GAStech_email_node.csv")
GAStech_edges <- read_csv("GAStech_email_edge-v2.csv")

Reviewing imported data

  • The plot
  • The code
Rows: 9,063
Columns: 8
$ source      <dbl> 43, 43, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 26, 26, 26…
$ target      <dbl> 41, 40, 51, 52, 53, 45, 44, 46, 48, 49, 47, 54, 27, 28, 29…
$ SentDate    <chr> "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014"…
$ SentTime    <time> 08:39:00, 08:39:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:0…
$ Subject     <chr> "GT-SeismicProcessorPro Bug Report", "GT-SeismicProcessorP…
$ MainSubject <chr> "Work related", "Work related", "Work related", "Work rela…
$ sourceLabel <chr> "Sven.Flecha", "Sven.Flecha", "Kanon.Herrero", "Kanon.Herr…
$ targetLabel <chr> "Isak.Baza", "Lucas.Alcazar", "Felix.Resumir", "Hideki.Coc…
glimpse(GAStech_edges)

Wrangling time

GAStech_edges <- GAStech_edges %>%
  mutate(SendDate = dmy(SentDate)) %>%
  mutate(Weekday = wday(SentDate,
                        label = TRUE,
                        abbr = FALSE))

Wrangling attributes

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(source, target, Weekday) %>%
    summarise(Weight = n()) %>%
  filter(source!=target) %>%
  filter(Weight > 1) %>%
  ungroup()

Creating network objects using tidygraph

2 articles for reading:

  1. https://www.data-imaginist.com/posts/2017-07-07-introducing-tidygraph/

  2. https://www.data-imaginist.com/posts/2018-02-12-tidygraph-1-1-a-tidy-hope/index.html

Main learning points:

tidygraph is for network/graph analysis in a tidyverse style. Instead of treating network data as one messy structure, it represents it as 2 tidy tables.

  1. Nodes table. This contains people, entities, items

  2. Edges table. This represents relationships/connections between them.

  3. tbl_graph is the main graph object. It is built on top of igraph, so it can use graph algorithms efficiently, but it is displayed and manipulated like tidy data.

  4. activate() switches between nodes and edges. There is a need to tell R whether now it is working on nodes and edges. activate(nodes) means apply subsequent adplyr verbs to node data, whole activate(edges) means they apply to edge data.

  5. tidygraph helps answer network questions, not just draw networks. An important point from the second article is that you do not need to plot a network to understand it.

  6. Centrality identifies important nodes. This can be used to help identify key actors in the communication/event network.

  7. Community detection groups related nodes. These detect groups of nodes that are more strongly connected to each other. This is useful for finding clusters of people, departments or suspicious communication groups.

  8. Morphing allows temporary graph transformation, using functions like morph(), convert().

Overall, the main lesson is that tidygraph lets you treat network data like tidy data. Therefore, networks can be represented as nodes and edges. More importantly, it helps you perform meaningful network analysis using centrality, community detection, path analysis and graph measures before visualising the results with ggraph.

Now, we will build tidygraph data model.

  • The plot
  • The code
# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Node Data: 54 × 4 (active)
      id label               Department     Title                               
   <dbl> <chr>               <chr>          <chr>                               
 1     1 Mat.Bramar          Administration Assistant to CEO                    
 2     2 Anda.Ribera         Administration Assistant to CFO                    
 3     3 Rachel.Pantanal     Administration Assistant to CIO                    
 4     4 Linda.Lagos         Administration Assistant to COO                    
 5     5 Ruscella.Mies.Haber Administration Assistant to Engineering Group Mana…
 6     6 Carla.Forluniau     Administration Assistant to IT Group Manager       
 7     7 Cornelia.Lais       Administration Assistant to Security Group Manager 
 8    44 Kanon.Herrero       Security       Badging Office                      
 9    45 Varja.Lagos         Security       Badging Office                      
10    46 Stenig.Fusil        Security       Building Control                    
# ℹ 44 more rows
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4
   from    to Weekday Weight
  <int> <int> <ord>    <int>
1     1     2 Sunday       5
2     1     2 Monday       2
3     1     2 Tuesday      3
# ℹ 1,369 more rows
GAStech_graph <- tbl_graph(nodes = GAStech_nodes,
                           edges = GAStech_edges_aggregated, 
                           directed = TRUE)
GAStech_graph

Reviewing output of the graph object

The output shows that GAStech_graph is an object representing a directed multigraph consisting of 54 nodes and 1372 edges. Each node represents an employee and contains 4 attributes: id, label, department and job title. The edge data captures interactions between employees, where the from and to columns indicate the source and destination nodes respectively. Each edge has a weekday attribute and weight attribute which represents the frequency or strength of interaction between the 2 employees that day. As the graph is a directed multigraph, multiple directed edges can exist between the same pair of employees, allowing interactions on different weekdays to be represented separately.

Changing the active object

  • The plot
  • The code
# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4 (active)
    from    to Weekday   Weight
   <int> <int> <ord>      <int>
 1    40    41 Saturday      13
 2    41    43 Monday        11
 3    35    31 Tuesday       10
 4    40    41 Monday        10
 5    40    43 Monday        10
 6    36    32 Sunday         9
 7    40    43 Saturday       9
 8    41    40 Monday         9
 9    19    15 Wednesday      8
10    35    38 Tuesday        8
# ℹ 1,362 more rows
#
# Node Data: 54 × 4
     id label           Department     Title           
  <dbl> <chr>           <chr>          <chr>           
1     1 Mat.Bramar      Administration Assistant to CEO
2     2 Anda.Ribera     Administration Assistant to CFO
3     3 Rachel.Pantanal Administration Assistant to CIO
# ℹ 51 more rows
GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  arrange(desc(Weight))

Plotting static network graphs

  • The plot
  • The code
ggraph(GAStech_graph) +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point()

Changing default network graph theme

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph) + 
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes())

g + theme_graph()

Changing the color

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph) + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(colour = 'grey50')) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = 'grey40'))

g + theme_graph(background = 'grey10',
                text_colour = 'white')

changing the layouts

library(tibble)
library(knitr)

layout_table <- tibble(
  Layout = c(
    "nicely", "fr", "kk", "lgl", "drl",
    "graphopt", "gem", "mds", "circle",
    "star", "grid", "randomly", "sphere", "dh"
  ),
  Purpose = c(
    "Default layout",
    "Force-directed",
    "Force-directed emphasizing distances",
    "Large graph layout",
    "Distributed recursive layout",
    "Energy-based optimization",
    "Force-directed",
    "Multidimensional scaling",
    "Circular arrangement",
    "Hub-and-spoke structures",
    "Rarely used for analysis",
    "Random baseline",
    "3D-like representation",
    "Davidson-Harel force-directed"
  ),
  `When to Use` = c(
    "General exploration",
    "Most common for social networks",
    "Small-medium networks",
    "Large networks (1000+ nodes)",
    "Very large networks",
    "Alternative to FR",
    "Alternative visualization",
    "Distance/similarity networks",
    "Showing sequences/groups",
    "Hub-centered networks",
    "Rarely used",
    "Baseline comparison",
    "Special visualizations",
    "Alternative force-directed layout"
  )
)

kable(
  layout_table,
  caption = "Common ggraph Layouts and Their Uses"
)
Common ggraph Layouts and Their Uses
Layout Purpose When to Use
nicely Default layout General exploration
fr Force-directed Most common for social networks
kk Force-directed emphasizing distances Small-medium networks
lgl Large graph layout Large networks (1000+ nodes)
drl Distributed recursive layout Very large networks
graphopt Energy-based optimization Alternative to FR
gem Force-directed Alternative visualization
mds Multidimensional scaling Distance/similarity networks
circle Circular arrangement Showing sequences/groups
star Hub-and-spoke structures Hub-centered networks
grid Rarely used for analysis Rarely used
randomly Random baseline Baseline comparison
sphere 3D-like representation Special visualizations
dh Davidson-Harel force-directed Alternative force-directed layout

Using the Fruchterman and Reingold layout

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes())

g + theme_graph()

Modifying network nodes

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department, 
                      size = 3))

g + theme_graph()

Modifying edges

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department), 
                  size = 3)

g + theme_graph()

Creating facet graphs

Faceting in ggraph is a technique used to split a network visualisation into multiple panels based on node or edge attributes. This helps reduce edge overplotting and makes complex network structures easier to interpret.

The 3 main faceting functions are: 1. facet_nodes() 2. facet_edges() 3. facet_graph()

Why faceting is useful: Faceting offers several advantages when analysing network data: 1. reduces visual clutter 2. highlights patterns 3. facilitates comparison 4. improves interpretability

A common mistake is to repeatedly filter the network and create separate plots.

Filtering Faceting
Produces separate plots Produces a coordinated set of panels
Layout may change between plots Layout remains consistent
Harder to compare Easier to compare
More code required More concise

facet_edges()

  • The plot
  • The code
set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department), 
                  size = 2)

g + facet_edges(~Weekday)

theme()

  • The plot
  • The code
set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department), 
                  size = 2) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')
  
g + facet_edges(~Weekday)

set_graph_style()

  • The plot
  • The code
set_graph_style() 

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department), 
                  size = 2)
  
g + facet_edges(~Weekday) +
  th_foreground(foreground = "grey80",  
                border = TRUE) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')

facet_nodes()

  • The plot
  • The code
set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph, 
            layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department), 
                  size = 2)
  
g + facet_nodes(~Department)+
  th_foreground(foreground = "grey80",  
                border = TRUE) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')

Network Metrics analysis

What is centrality? Centrality measures are network analysis metrics that quantify how important, influential or strategically positioned a node is within a network. Different measures capture different aspects of importance, so a node that ranks highly on one metric may not highly on another.

  1. Degree centrality: Degree centrality measures the number of direct connections a node has.

  2. Betweenness centrality: Betweenness centrality measures how frequently a node lies on the shortest paths between other nodes.

  3. Closenes centrality: Closeness centrality measures how close a node is to all other nodes in the network based on shortest path distances.

  4. Eigenvector centrality: Eigenvector centrality extends degree centrality by considering not only how many connections a node has, but also how important those connections are.

Measure Captures High Score Indicates
Degree Direct connections Highly connected
Betweenness Control of paths Broker or bridge
Closeness Distance to others Efficient communicator
Eigenvector Connection quality Influential or prestigious

Insights: The “most connected” person is not always the most important. A node can have high degree and low betweenness,if it communicates with many people inside a single department. A node can have lower degree and higher betweenness, if it serves as the only bridge between departments.

Question Recommended Metric
Who talks to the most people? Degree
Who connects different groups? Betweenness
Who can spread information fastest? Closeness
Who is connected to influential people? Eigenvector

Computing centrality indices

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(betweenness_centrality = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department,
            size=betweenness_centrality))
g + theme_graph()

visualising network metrics

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department, 
                      size = centrality_betweenness()))
g + theme_graph()

Visualising community

  • The plot
  • The code
g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_edge_betweenness(
      weights = Weight, 
      directed = TRUE))) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") + 
  geom_edge_link(
    aes(
      width=Weight), 
    alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(
    range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(
    aes(colour = community))  

g + theme_graph()
  • The plot
  • The code
g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_optimal(weights = Weight)),
         betweenness_measure = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_mark_hull(
    aes(x, y, 
        group = community, 
        fill = community),  
    alpha = 0.2,  
    expand = unit(0.3, "cm"),  # Expand
    radius = unit(0.3, "cm")  # Smoothness
  ) + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(fill = Department,
                      size = betweenness_measure),
                      color = "black",
                      shape = 21)
  
g + theme_graph()

Building interactive network graph with visNetwork

Data preparation

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  left_join(GAStech_nodes, by = c("sourceLabel" = "label")) %>%
  rename(from = id) %>%
  left_join(GAStech_nodes, by = c("targetLabel" = "label")) %>%
  rename(to = id) %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(from, to) %>%
    summarise(weight = n()) %>%
  filter(from!=to) %>%
  filter(weight > 1) %>%
  ungroup()

Plotting the first interactive network graph

visNetwork(GAStech_nodes, 
           GAStech_edges_aggregated)

Working with layout

  • The plot
  • The code
visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr")

Working with visual attributes

GAStech_nodes <- GAStech_nodes %>%
  rename(group = Department)

Assigning colours

  • The plot
  • The code
visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)

visual attributes - edges

  • The plot
  • The code
visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visEdges(arrows = "to", 
           smooth = list(enabled = TRUE, 
                         type = "curvedCW")) %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)

Interactivity

  • The plot
  • The code
visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE,
             nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)

Alternatives

Arc diagram

  • The plot
  • The code
library(ggraph)

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(
    degree = centrality_degree()
  ) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "linear") +

  geom_edge_arc(
    aes(width = Weight),
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +

  geom_node_point(
    aes(size = degree,
        colour = Department)
  ) +

  geom_node_text(
    aes(label = label),
    angle = 90,
    hjust = 1,
    size = 2
  ) +

  theme_graph()

Communication appears to be highly concentrated around a small number of employees, indicating a hub-and-spoke network structure rather than evenly distributed interactions.

Hive plot (ggraph)

  • The plot
  • The code
library(ggraph)

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(
    degree = centrality_degree()
  ) %>%
  ggraph(
    layout = "hive",
    axis = Department,
    sort.by = degree
  ) +

  geom_edge_hive(
    alpha = 0.2
  ) +

  geom_node_point(
    aes(size = degree,
        colour = Department)
  ) +

  theme_graph()

Communication occurs extensively across departments rather than remaining within specific departments, indicating a highly interconnected organisation.

Matrix network plot

  • The plot
  • The code
library(ggraph)

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(
    community = group_infomap()
  ) %>%
  ggraph(
    layout = "matrix"
  ) +

  geom_edge_point(
    aes(size = Weight,
        colour = Weight)
  ) +

  theme_graph()

The communication network exhibits distinct clusters of densely connected employees. This shows the presence of communities that interact more frequently within the group than with the rest of the organisation.

Community bubble network

  • The plot
  • The code
library(ggforce)

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(
    community = as.factor(group_infomap()),
    degree = centrality_degree()
  ) %>%

  ggraph(layout = "fr") +

  geom_mark_hull(
    aes(
      x = x,
      y = y,
      group = community,
      fill = community
    ),
    alpha = 0.15
  ) +

  geom_edge_link(
    alpha = 0.2
  ) +

  geom_node_point(
    aes(
      size = degree,
      colour = Department
    )
  ) +

  theme_graph()

The organisation contains several distinct communication communities, but a small number of highly connected employees act as bridges linking these communities together.
